ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Szechuan Mala Tang. Mapped to the nearest parent class 'soup food product' (FOODON_03315595) as an approximate anchor. A more specific term for spiced Chinese broth does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Soups and broths) is the closest defensible category for Mala Tang broth. No Szechuan-specific or spiced-broth subcategory exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Szechuan Mala Tang or equivalent spiced Chinese broth as of 2024.

main_ingredients: various optional add-ins: thinly sliced meats, leafy vegetables, mushrooms, tofu, noodles
allergens: gluten (possible from noodles or bean paste)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Szechuan Spicy Broth (Mala Tang) is approximately 500 g, representing one bowl as typically served at street food stalls or casual restaurants in China.

nutrient_source.title: Soups, broth, beef, ready-to-serve — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented by FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for spiced broth analogues
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Szechuan Mala Tang broth exists in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA tables. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry for beef broth/soup (FDC ID 171532) and spiced soup analogues in FAO/INFOODS SEA, adjusted for the high chili oil and doubanjiang content characteristic of Mala Tang. Sodium is elevated to reflect concentrated fermented bean paste. Fat is elevated to reflect chili oil base. Vitamin A is elevated to reflect chili pepper content. Input AI-generated values were cross-checked against these analogues and retained where consistent. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 30% of total fat based on chili oil and lard composition typical of Szechuan broth. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions and moisture data for Szechuan Mala Tang broth are not available in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA as of 2024.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed vegetable and noodle soup
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Szechuan Mala Tang broth. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on mixed vegetable and noodle soup entries in the University of Sydney GI Database (GI range 38–52 for comparable Asian mixed soups). The low GI reflects the high fat and protein content of the broth base, which slows gastric emptying. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 34.0) / 100 = 15.3. Carbohydrate per 500 g serving = 6.8 × 5 = 34.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (450 mg per 100 g; 2,250 mg per 500 g serving), exceeding the WHO daily recommended limit of 2,000 mg in a single serving
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Szechuan Spicy Broth due to its extremely high sodium load from doubanjiang and concentrated broth base, which can acutely raise blood pressure; if consumed, portion size should be reduced to no more than 100–150 g and paired with low-sodium foods for the remainder of the day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate content (6.8 g per 100 g) with a low glycemic index of approximately 45, but glycemic load per standard 500 g serving reaches 15.3, which is moderate
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can consume Szechuan Spicy Broth in moderation, favouring the broth base with non-starchy vegetables and lean protein add-ins while limiting or avoiding noodles and starchy vegetables to keep the glycemic load within target; blood glucose monitoring after consumption is advised given variability in add-in ingredients.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (8.5 g per 100 g) with estimated saturated fat of 2.6 g per 100 g, combined with very high sodium (450 mg per 100 g), both of which are established cardiovascular risk factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Szechuan Spicy Broth to occasional small portions due to its high sodium and fat content, particularly the saturated fat from chili oil and lard typically used in the broth base, which can adversely affect blood pressure and lipid profiles; choosing a lighter broth variant with reduced oil and lean protein add-ins is recommended.

gerd_acid_reflux.key_factor: High chili and Szechuan peppercorn content can trigger or worsen gastroesophageal reflux symptoms
gerd_acid_reflux.guidance: Individuals with GERD or acid reflux should avoid Szechuan Spicy Broth as the high capsaicin and spice load is a well-documented trigger for reflux symptoms.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Szechuan Spicy Broth, known in Mandarin as Mala Tang (麻辣烫 / 麻辣燙), is a signature dish of Sichuan cuisine originating from Sichuan Province, China. The name derives from 'ma' (麻, numbing) and 'la' (辣, spicy), describing the distinctive dual sensation produced by Szechuan peppercorns and dried chili peppers. The broth is prepared by simmering a complex base of Szechuan peppercorns, dried chili peppers, doubanjiang (fermented broad bean and chili paste), ginger, garlic, star anise, and other aromatics in oil and stock, creating a deeply flavoured, fiery, and aromatic liquid. Mala Tang is served as a customisable hot pot-style soup in which diners select add-in ingredients — commonly thinly sliced beef or pork, leafy greens, mushrooms, tofu, fish balls, and glass or wheat noodles — which are cooked directly in the broth. The dish is a popular street food and casual restaurant staple across China and increasingly in Southeast Asia and globally. Nutritionally, the broth base per 100 g provides approximately 115 kcal, 2.5 g protein, 8.5 g fat, and 6.8 g carbohydrate. Sodium content is very high at 450 mg per 100 g, making a standard 500 g serving deliver approximately 2,250 mg sodium, which exceeds the WHO daily recommended limit. The high fat content reflects the chili oil and lard base. Vitamin A content is notable at approximately 54 mcg RAE per 100 g, contributed by the chili pepper content. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low), though the glycemic load per standard serving is moderate at approximately 15.3. Clinically, this dish is of high concern for individuals with hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its sodium and fat load, and of moderate concern for those with Type 2 Diabetes depending on add-in ingredient choices. It is not suitable for individuals with GERD or sensitive gastrointestinal conditions.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Mala Tang?
Is Mala Tang high in sodium?
What is the glycemic index of Szechuan broth?
Is Mala Tang suitable for hypertension?
What are the ingredients in Szechuan spicy broth?
Is Mala Tang the same as hot pot?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Szechuan Mala Tang broth exists in any approved primary source (USDA FoodData Central, FAO/INFOODS SEA, HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy beef broth and spiced soup analogues in FAO/INFOODS SEA, cross-checked against the AI-generated input values which were internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable Asian mixed soups. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions and moisture data for Szechuan Mala Tang broth are not available in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA as of 2024. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is set to 0.0 as this is a plant-oil and vegetable-based broth with no significant animal-derived vitamin D source; confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy soup analogues. 3. Vitamin A input value of 650 IU has been converted to approximately 54.2 mcg RAE (using the standard conversion factor of 1 mcg RAE = 12 IU for plant-source beta-carotene from chili peppers) and reported as vitamin_a_mcg_rae. 4. Saturated fat of 2.6 g per 100 g is estimated at approximately 30% of total fat (8.5 g), consistent with the chili oil and lard composition typical of Szechuan broth base, cross-referenced against USDA SR Legacy entries for chili oil and lard. 5. The input source (Szechuan University West China School of Public Health) is not on the approved source list and has not been used as primary evidence; USDA FoodData Central and FAO/INFOODS SEA are used instead. 6. The portions array in the input contained a duplicate entry (two identical 500 g servings); only one 500 g serving is retained. 7. This is a highly customisable dish; actual nutrient values will vary significantly depending on add-in ingredients chosen by the diner. 8. Glycemic load per serving calculated as GL = (45 × 34.0) / 100 = 15.3, where 34.0 g is the carbohydrate content of a 500 g serving (6.8 g × 5).
